It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 179 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 14 actions returned the board to a state already seen 14 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 14 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 8 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Game over again on (33, 21). This is consistently causing game over. Let me reconsider the problem.
The block at (33, 21) is directly to the right of the player's column (15). The player is at cols 14-16. The block at (33, 21) is at the bottom row.
Wait - looking at the board image, I see the player (blue/yellow) is positioned at the bottom left area. The block at (33, 21) is just to the right of the player. Maybe the issue is that the player is physically blocking access to that block?
Let me think about this differently. What if I need to MOVE the player to a different position before clicking certain blocks? The player can move LEFT and RIGHT.
The player is currently at cols 14-16 (approximately). If I move the player to the right, maybe I can then click (33, 21) from a different position, or maybe the player needs to be OUT OF THE WAY to click that block.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the whole puzzle. What if the goal is NOT to click all blocks? What if some blocks are "traps" that cause game over?
Looking at the pattern:
- Col 15 blocks: aligned with player, clickable
- Col 21 blocks: (27, 21) clickable, (33, 21) causes game over
- Cols 27-51: all clickable
What if the goal is to clear ALL blocks EXCEPT the ones that would cause game over? Maybe (33, 21) is a "trap" block that shouldn't be clicked?
But then how do I clear the level? Let me try a different approach: what if I need to move the player first?
Let me try moving RIGHT to shift the player, then see if (33, 21) becomes clickable.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# (33, 21) consistently causes game over. New hypothesis: need to MOVE player before clicking it.
# Player is at cols ~14-16. Block at col 21 is just right of player.
# Try moving player RIGHT to get out of the way, then click (33, 21).

# First check current player position
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
player = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"Player nodes: {len(player)}")
for n in player:
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  color={n['color']}, center=({sum(rows)/len(rows):.0f}, {sum(cols)/len(cols):.0f}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Also check yellow
yellow = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'Y']
for n in yellow:
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  yellow: center=({sum(rows)/len(rows):.0f}, {sum(cols)/len(cols):.0f})")

# Try moving RIGHT a few times then clicking (33, 21)
action([
    'RIGHT',
    'RIGHT',
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 21},
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  yellow: center=(11, 16)
  yellow: center=(11, 20)
  yellow: center=(11, 22)
  yellow: center=(11, 26)
  yellow: center=(11, 28)
  yellow: center=(38, 22)
